sections.title: Nicht — Negating Verbs and Adjectives
sections.explanation: German uses 'nicht' to negate verbs, adjectives, adverbs, and specific noun phrases with a definite article. 'Nicht' is not a simple one-to-one replacement for English 'not' — its position matters. General rule: 'nicht' comes at the end of the clause (or before a predicate adjective/adverb, or before an infinitive at the end). Before a predicate adjective: 'Das Wetter ist nicht schön.' (The weather is not nice.) Before a specific noun phrase with a definite article: 'Ich kenne den Mann nicht.' (I don't know the man.) Before an infinitive: 'Er kann nicht kommen.' (He can't come.)
sections.tip: Place 'nicht' at the end, or just before whatever is being negated (adjective, infinitive, prepositional phrase). When in doubt: nicht at the end.

examples.en: I am not going shopping today.

exercises.prompt: Where does 'nicht' go to negate a simple statement like 'Ich verstehe das'?
options: At the end: 'Ich verstehe das nicht.'
Before the verb: 'Ich nicht verstehe das.'
After the subject: 'Ich nicht verstehe das.'
exercises.explanation: For a simple verb negation, 'nicht' goes at the end: 'Ich verstehe das nicht.' (I don't understand that.)

exercises.prompt: How do you negate 'Das ist schön' (That is beautiful)?
exercises.explanation: When negating a predicate adjective, 'nicht' comes directly before it: 'Das ist nicht schön.' (That is not beautiful.)

exercises.prompt: Er kommt morgen ___. (He is not coming tomorrow.)
exercises.explanation: 'Nicht' at the end negates the whole verb phrase. 'Er kommt morgen nicht.'

exercises.prompt: Negate: 'Sie wohnt in Berlin.' (She lives in Berlin.)
exercises.explanation: When negating a prepositional phrase or specific detail, 'nicht' comes before it: 'Sie wohnt nicht in Berlin.'

exercises.prompt: Match each sentence to its correct negation:
exercises.explanation: Nicht placement: before adjective (nicht gut), at clause end (kommt nicht, versteht das nicht), before the specific element being negated.

exercises.prompt: Where does 'nicht' go with modal verbs: 'Er kann kommen' → He can't come?
options: Before the infinitive: 'Er kann nicht kommen.'
At the very end: 'Er kann kommen nicht.'
After the subject: 'Er nicht kann kommen.'
exercises.explanation: With modal + infinitive, 'nicht' comes just before the infinitive at the end: 'Er kann nicht kommen.'

exercises.prompt: Das Essen schmeckt ___ gut. (The food does not taste good.)
exercises.explanation: 'Nicht' before a predicate adjective: 'Das Essen schmeckt nicht gut.' (The food doesn't taste good.)

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I am not sleeping'?
exercises.explanation: Simple verb negation: 'nicht' at the end. 'Ich schlafe nicht.' Note: German present tense covers both 'I sleep' and 'I am sleeping'.

sections.title: Kein/Keine — Negating Nouns
sections.explanation: Use 'kein' (not 'nicht') to negate nouns that would normally take the indefinite article (ein/eine) or no article at all. 'kein' declines exactly like 'ein': kein (masc. nom.), keine (fem. nom.), kein (neut. nom.), keine (plural). Examples: 'Ich habe ein Auto.' → 'Ich habe kein Auto.' (I have no car.) 'Sie trinkt Kaffee.' → 'Sie trinkt keinen Kaffee.' (She drinks no coffee.) 'Wir haben Zeit.' → 'Wir haben keine Zeit.' (We have no time.) Remember: 'nicht' negates verbs/adjectives; 'kein' negates nouns with indefinite/no article.
sections.tip: kein = 'not a' or 'no'. Use it instead of 'nicht ein'. kein follows the same endings as ein: keine (fem./pl.), keinen (masc. acc.).

examples.en: He has no money. (das Geld → kein)
We have no time. (die Zeit → keine)
I drink no coffee. (masc. acc. → keinen)
I have no car. (das Auto → kein)

exercises.prompt: When do you use 'kein' instead of 'nicht'?
options: To negate nouns that would use 'ein/eine' or no article
exercises.explanation: 'kein' negates nouns that normally take an indefinite article (ein/eine) or no article at all. 'nicht' negates verbs and adjectives.

exercises.prompt: How do you negate 'Ich habe ein Auto' (I have a car)?
exercises.explanation: Replace 'ein' with 'kein': Ich habe kein Auto. (I have no car / I don't have a car.)

exercises.prompt: Wir haben ___ Zeit. (We have no time.) — Zeit is feminine
exercises.explanation: 'Zeit' is feminine (die Zeit) → keine. Wir haben keine Zeit.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form for 'I drink no coffee'? (Kaffee = der, accusative)
exercises.explanation: 'Kaffee' is masculine (der Kaffee). As accusative object: kein→keinen. Ich trinke keinen Kaffee.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun phrase to its correct kein-form:
exercises.explanation: kein (masc./neut.), keine (fem./pl.) in nominative. kein follows ein-endings exactly.

exercises.prompt: Negate: 'Er hat Geschwister.' (He has siblings.)
exercises.explanation: 'Geschwister' (siblings) is a plural noun with no article → use 'keine': Er hat keine Geschwister.

exercises.prompt: Ich habe ___ Hunger. (I am not hungry.) — Hunger is masc., no article
exercises.explanation: 'Hunger' is masculine (der Hunger) and used without an article. As accusative: kein→keinen. Ich habe keinen Hunger.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is correct for 'She doesn't speak German'?
options: Both are correct, but kein is preferred here
exercises.explanation: Both 'Sie spricht kein Deutsch' (Deutsch as a noun, no article) and 'Sie spricht nicht Deutsch' (negating the verb phrase) are used. 'Kein Deutsch sprechen' is the more common idiomatic form.

sections.title: Nicht vs. Kein — Deciding Which to Use
sections.explanation: The choice between 'nicht' and 'kein' depends on what is being negated. Use 'kein/keine' when negating a noun phrase with an indefinite article (ein→kein) or a noun used without an article (bare noun → kein). Use 'nicht' for everything else: negating a verb action, an adjective, an adverb, or a noun phrase with a DEFINITE article (der/die/das). Example: 'Ich lese nicht.' (I don't read — verb negation.) 'Ich lese das Buch nicht.' (I'm not reading the book — definite article noun + nicht.) 'Ich habe kein Buch.' (I have no book — indefinite article → kein.)
sections.tip: Quick test: Can you replace what you're negating with 'a/an something'? → kein. Otherwise → nicht.

examples.en: I have no money. (bare noun → kein)
I don't know the teacher. (definite noun → nicht)
She has no idea. (indef. article → kein)

exercises.prompt: Which is correct: 'I am not tired'?
exercises.explanation: 'müde' is an adjective → use 'nicht'. Kein/keine are only for nouns.

exercises.prompt: Which is correct: 'I have no dog'? (Hund = masc.)
exercises.explanation: Negating 'ein Hund' → replace 'ein' with 'kein': keinen Hund (accusative masculine).

exercises.prompt: Negate: 'Ich kenne die Frau.' (I know the woman.) — definite article
exercises.explanation: When the noun has a DEFINITE article (die Frau), use 'nicht' at the end: 'Ich kenne die Frau nicht.'

exercises.prompt: Choose nicht or kein for each sentence:
exercises.explanation: nicht: adjective (nicht glücklich), definite-article noun or verb (nicht + verb/adjective). kein/keine: noun without or with indef. article.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Das ist ___ Problem.' (That is not a problem.) — Problem is neuter
exercises.explanation: Negating 'ein Problem' (a problem) → use 'kein': Das ist kein Problem. Neuter noun → kein (not keine).
